news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in advanced molecular analysis, innovative nanophotonic frequency combs, and surprising discoveries about animal behavior.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine de l'analyse moléculaire de pointe, des peignes de fréquences nanophotoniques innovants et des découvertes surprenantes sur le comportement animal.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances en el análisis molecular avanzado, innovadores peines de frecuencia nanofotónicos y sorprendentes descubrimientos sobre el comportamiento animal.

articles.title: Most mass spectrometers handle a few molecules. This new prototype handles billions
articles.summary: A new prototype could supercharge mass spectrometry by analyzing thousands of molecules at once instead of one by one. The breakthrough boosts sensitivity enough to reveal rare, hidden molecules—potentially transforming fields like drug discovery and single-cell biology.
articles.content:
Mass spectrometry is a widely used technique that helps scientists determine what molecules are present in a sample and how much of each there is. However, most current instruments examine molecules one at a time or in very small groups. This approach can be slow, expensive, and prone to missing rare but important molecules hidden among more abundant ones.

A more advanced version of this technology could eventually allow researchers to capture the complete molecular makeup of a single cell, monitor thousands of chemical reactions simultaneously, and speed up processes such as drug discovery.

A new study describes an early step toward that goal. Researchers have developed a prototype called MultiQ-IT that can process large numbers of molecules at the same time. The work provides a framework for building faster and more sensitive instruments, potentially enabling a shift similar to the transformations seen in genomics and computing.

"What revolutionized DNA sequencing wasn't any change in the underlying chemistry. That's remained fundamentally the same," says Brian T. Chait, Laboratory of Mass Spectrometry and Gaseous Ion Chemistry at Rockefeller. "It was the ability to run so many chemical reactions in parallel, which took genome sequencing from a billion-dollar effort to something that costs around $100. The same thing happened in computing with GPUs. And that's what we're trying to do with mass spectrometry."

The Bottleneck in Modern Mass Spectrometry

Mass spectrometry dates back to around 1913 and has become one of biology's most important analytical methods. It works by ionizing molecules, meaning giving them an electric charge, and then measuring their mass-to-charge ratio to identify and quantify them. Despite its capabilities, most systems still operate sequentially, analyzing only one or a few types of ions at a time. This limits their ability to detect rare molecules in complex biological samples.

"It's a wonderful technique -- you can do unimaginably wonderful, analytical things with it," Chait says. "But I was always a little frustrated by its limitations. I knew, in my heart, it could be better."

Improving this limitation could have a major impact on fields such as single-cell proteomics and metabolomics, which aim to measure all proteins or metabolites within a single cell. Unlike DNA, these molecules cannot be copied or amplified, and some may be millions of times less abundant than others. While mass spectrometry is already used in these areas, its current sensitivity often falls short when trying to detect faint signals among overwhelming background noise.

To address this challenge, Chait and his team believed the solution would be "massive parallelization," a concept that previously transformed computing and DNA sequencing. In computing, breaking large problems into many smaller tasks and processing them simultaneously with graphics processing units, or GPUs, led to major performance gains. DNA sequencing followed a similar path, allowing millions of reactions to be analyzed at once at much lower cost.

"It was a very obvious idea," says Andrew Krutchinsky, a senior research associate in the lab. "But how to do it with mass spectrometry wasn't obvious."

A Parallel Approach Inspired by Cells

The concept behind MultiQ-IT emerged from long-term research on how molecules move in and out of a cell's nucleus through structures known as nuclear pore complexes. These structures distribute traffic across many small openings instead of forcing everything through a single path. The researchers wondered if mass spectrometry could be redesigned to work in a similar way.

The result is a newly designed ion-trapping chamber intended to replace a key part of traditional mass spectrometers. This cube-shaped device contains hundreds of small, electrically controlled openings. Inside the chamber, ions collide with gas molecules, slow down, and move randomly. This allows the system to sort, hold, and direct multiple groups of ions at the same time instead of processing them sequentially.

The team expanded the design from just six openings to more than 1,000, testing how effectively ions could be managed and separated. They showed that a single incoming stream of ions could be divided into multiple parallel streams for simultaneous analysis.

Handling Billions of Molecules at Once

The prototype delivered impressive performance. A version with 486 ports could hold up to ten billion charges at once, which is about a thousand times more than conventional ion traps.

The system also improves detection by allowing common background molecules to escape while keeping rarer, more informative ones inside. This increased the signal-to-noise ratio by as much as 100-fold, making it possible to detect proteins that were previously undetectable. To achieve this, the researchers applied a small electrical voltage barrier at the exits of the trap. Singly charged ions could escape, while multiply charged ions, which are often more biologically important, remained trapped.

In a larger design with 1,134 ports, only 39 open ports were needed to reach half of the system's maximum filtering efficiency, similar to how cells use a limited number of nuclear pores to manage molecular traffic. The researchers also found that spreading ions across many channels reduces the strong electrical repulsion that occurs when large numbers of similarly charged particles are packed into a small space.

This boost in sensitivity could improve the detection of low abundance crosslinked peptides, which are valuable for mapping the structures of large protein complexes. "The least abundant things can be more important than the more abundant things," Krutchinsky says.

A Blueprint for Future Instruments

At this stage, MultiQ-IT is not yet a finished commercial product but rather a proof of concept showing what is achievable. The researchers see it as a foundational design that could eventually be developed into practical tools for clinical and laboratory use.

"There was a lot of development between the discovery of a reaction for sequencing DNA and modern genomics; decades between the first transistor and putting a billion transistors on a chip," Chait says. "In both cases, someone first had to show it could be done, and then industry took over. I think we've shown one way mass spectrometry can be done more efficiently."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in mass spectrometry that enables parallel processing of billions of molecules, vastly improving sensitivity and speed. This advancement has broad implications for drug discovery, single-cell biology, and molecular analysis, potentially transforming multiple scientific fields and benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context and explains the innovation's potential impact clearly.
articles.personality_title: New mass spectrometer prototype analyzes billions of molecules at once
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mass spectrometry is a tool that helps scientists identify and measure molecules in samples. Usually, these machines look at molecules one by one or in very small groups, which can be slow and miss rare molecules.

**What happened** – Researchers created a new prototype called MultiQ-IT that can analyze thousands of molecules simultaneously. This device uses hundreds of tiny openings to trap and separate molecules at the same time, allowing it to handle up to ten billion charged particles at once—about a thousand times more than current machines.

**Impact** – This technology can detect rare molecules that older machines might miss because it reduces background noise and improves sensitivity by up to 100 times. It could help scientists better understand single cells and speed up drug discovery by revealing important molecules hidden in complex samples.

**What's next step** – The prototype is a first step, not yet ready for commercial use. Researchers hope to develop it further into practical tools for labs and clinics, similar to how DNA sequencing advanced over decades from early ideas to widely used technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new mass spectrometer design can analyze billions of molecules at once, greatly improving the speed and sensitivity of molecular detection for science and medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouveau prototype de spectromètre de masse analyse des milliards de molécules simultanément
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La spectrométrie de masse est un outil qui aide les scientifiques à identifier et mesurer les molécules dans un échantillon. Habituellement, ces appareils examinent les molécules une par une ou en petits groupes, ce qui peut être lent et manquer des molécules rares.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un nouveau prototype appelé MultiQ-IT capable d'analyser des milliers de molécules en même temps. Cet appareil utilise des centaines de petites ouvertures pour piéger et séparer les molécules simultanément, pouvant gérer jusqu'à dix milliards de particules chargées, soit environ mille fois plus que les machines actuelles.

**Impact** – Cette technologie peut détecter des molécules rares que les anciens appareils pouvaient manquer, car elle réduit le bruit de fond et améliore la sensibilité jusqu'à 100 fois. Elle pourrait aider les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les cellules individuelles et accélérer la découverte de médicaments en révélant des molécules importantes cachées dans des échantillons complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le prototype est une première étape, pas encore prêt pour une utilisation commerciale. Les chercheurs espèrent le développer pour en faire des outils pratiques pour les laboratoires et les cliniques, comme la séquence ADN qui a évolué sur plusieurs décennies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau spectromètre de masse peut analyser des milliards de molécules simultanément, améliorant grandement la vitesse et la sensibilité de détection moléculaire pour la science et la médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo prototipo de espectrómetro de masas analiza miles de millones de moléculas a la vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La espectrometría de masas es una herramienta que ayuda a los científicos a identificar y medir moléculas en una muestra. Normalmente, estas máquinas analizan las moléculas una por una o en grupos pequeños, lo que puede ser lento y perder moléculas raras.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon un nuevo prototipo llamado MultiQ-IT que puede analizar miles de moléculas simultáneamente. Este dispositivo usa cientos de pequeñas aberturas para atrapar y separar moléculas al mismo tiempo, pudiendo manejar hasta diez mil millones de partículas cargadas, unas mil veces más que las máquinas actuales.

**Impacto** – Esta tecnología puede detectar moléculas raras que las máquinas antiguas podrían pasar por alto, porque reduce el ruido de fondo y mejora la sensibilidad hasta 100 veces. Podría ayudar a los científicos a entender mejor las células individuales y acelerar el descubrimiento de medicamentos al revelar moléculas importantes ocultas en muestras complejas.

**Próximo paso** – El prototipo es un primer paso, aún no listo para uso comercial. Los investigadores esperan desarrollarlo en herramientas prácticas para laboratorios y clínicas, como ocurrió con la secuenciación del ADN que evolucionó durante décadas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo diseño de espectrómetro de masas puede analizar miles de millones de moléculas a la vez, mejorando mucho la velocidad y sensibilidad de la detección molecular para la ciencia y la medicina.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, cube-shaped chamber filled with hundreds of small, softly illuminated portals, each releasing streams of colorful, gently swirling ions flowing outward like vibrant threads of light, symbolizing the parallel processing of billions of molecules inspired by cellular nuclear pores, set against a calm, neutral background with natural, muted tones.

articles.title: Topological soliton frequency comb in nanophotonic lithium niobate
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10292-2An integrated lithium niobate–semiconductor platform generates topological soliton frequency combs on-chip, enabling turn-key, dispersion-agnostic comb sources without high-Q resonators or complex stabilization.
articles.content: Frequency combs have revolutionized metrology, ranging and optical clocks1, motivating substantial efforts on the development of chip-scale comb sources2,3. Some on-chip comb sources exist and have been implemented through electro-optic modulation4,5, mode-locked lasers6,7, quantum cascade lasers8,9,10 or soliton formation by Kerr nonlinearity11,12. However, widespread deployment of on-chip comb sources has remained elusive, as they still require radiofrequency sources, high-Q (high-quality factor) resonators or complex stabilization schemes while facing efficiency challenges. Here, we demonstrate an on-chip frequency comb source based on the integration of a lithium niobate nanophotonic circuit with a semiconductor laser that can alleviate these challenges. We show the formation of temporal topological solitons in an on-chip nanophotonic parametric oscillator with quadratic nonlinearity and low finesse. These solitons, independent of the dispersion regime, consist of phase defects separating two π-out-of-phase continuous wave solutions at the signal frequency, which is half the input pump frequency13,14. We use on-chip cross-correlation for temporal measurements and confirm formation of topological solitons as short as 60 fs around 2 μm, in agreement with a generalized parametrically forced Ginzburg–Landau theory15,16,17. Moreover, we demonstrate a proof-of-concept turn-key operation of a hybrid-integrated source of topological frequency comb. Topological solitons are potential candidates for the development of integrated comb sources, which are dispersion-sign agnostic and do not require high-Q resonators or high-speed modulators, and can provide access to hard-to-reach spectral regions, including mid-infrared regions18.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in integrated photonics technology with the demonstration of a novel on-chip frequency comb source using topological solitons in lithium niobate nanophotonics. This innovation overcomes major challenges such as the need for high-Q resonators and complex stabilization, enabling more practical, efficient, and versatile comb sources. The technology has broad implications for metrology, optical clocks, and accessing difficult spectral regions, indicating substantial real-world impact and significance.
articles.personality_title: New on-chip laser technology simplifies precise light signals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Frequency combs are special light signals that help in measuring time and distance very accurately. They are used in things like optical clocks and scientific instruments. Usually, making these combs on small chips is hard because the devices need complex parts and careful control.

**What happened** – Scientists created a new device that makes frequency combs on a tiny chip using a material called lithium niobate combined with a small laser. This device produces special light pulses called topological solitons without needing complicated parts like high-quality resonators or extra electronic controls. These solitons are very stable and short, lasting only 60 femtoseconds (that is 0.00000000000006 seconds).

**Impact** – This breakthrough means that frequency combs can become easier to make and use in smaller, simpler devices. It removes the need for difficult parts and complex setups, allowing the technology to work across a wider range of light colors, including those hard to reach before, like mid-infrared light. This could help improve scientific tools and technologies that rely on precise light signals.

**What's next step** – Researchers will work on making these devices more practical and ready for real-world use. They might develop new sensors, clocks, or communication tools that use this technology, expanding its benefits to many fields.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have built a simpler, more efficient chip that creates precise light signals using topological solitons, opening new possibilities for advanced optical technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle technologie laser sur puce simplifie les signaux lumineux précis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les peignes de fréquences sont des signaux lumineux spéciaux qui aident à mesurer le temps et la distance avec une grande précision. Ils sont utilisés dans des horloges optiques et des instruments scientifiques. Habituellement, fabriquer ces peignes sur de petites puces est difficile car les appareils nécessitent des pièces complexes et un contrôle précis.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé un nouveau dispositif qui produit des peignes de fréquences sur une puce minuscule en utilisant un matériau appelé niobate de lithium combiné à un petit laser. Ce dispositif génère des impulsions lumineuses spéciales appelées solitons topologiques sans avoir besoin de pièces compliquées comme des résonateurs de haute qualité ou des contrôles électroniques supplémentaires. Ces solitons sont très stables et très courts, durant seulement 60 femtosecondes (soit 0,00000000000006 secondes).

**Impact** – Cette avancée signifie que les peignes de fréquences peuvent devenir plus faciles à fabriquer et à utiliser dans des appareils plus petits et plus simples. Elle supprime le besoin de pièces difficiles et de configurations complexes, permettant à la technologie de fonctionner sur une gamme plus large de couleurs lumineuses, y compris celles difficiles à atteindre auparavant, comme la lumière dans l’infrarouge moyen. Cela pourrait améliorer les outils scientifiques et les technologies qui dépendent de signaux lumineux précis.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont travailler à rendre ces dispositifs plus pratiques et prêts pour une utilisation réelle. Ils pourraient développer de nouveaux capteurs, horloges ou outils de communication utilisant cette technologie, étendant ses avantages à de nombreux domaines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont construit une puce plus simple et efficace qui crée des signaux lumineux précis grâce aux solitons topologiques, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour les technologies optiques avancées.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva tecnología láser en chip simplifica señales de luz precisas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los peines de frecuencia son señales de luz especiales que ayudan a medir el tiempo y la distancia con mucha precisión. Se usan en relojes ópticos y equipos científicos. Normalmente, hacer estos peines en chips pequeños es difícil porque los dispositivos necesitan piezas complejas y control cuidadoso.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos crearon un nuevo dispositivo que produce peines de frecuencia en un chip muy pequeño usando un material llamado niobato de litio junto con un láser pequeño. Este dispositivo genera pulsos de luz especiales llamados solitones topológicos sin necesitar partes complicadas como resonadores de alta calidad o controles electrónicos extras. Estos solitones son muy estables y cortos, duran solo 60 femtosegundos (eso es 0,00000000000006 segundos).

**Impacto** – Este avance significa que los peines de frecuencia pueden hacerse más fáciles de fabricar y usar en dispositivos más pequeños y simples. Elimina la necesidad de partes difíciles y configuraciones complejas, permitiendo que la tecnología funcione en un rango más amplio de colores de luz, incluyendo aquellos difíciles de alcanzar antes, como la luz en el infrarrojo medio. Esto podría mejorar herramientas científicas y tecnologías que dependen de señales de luz precisas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajarán para hacer estos dispositivos más prácticos y listos para su uso real. Podrían desarrollar nuevos sensores, relojes o herramientas de comunicación que usen esta tecnología, extendiendo sus beneficios a muchos campos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han creado un chip más simple y eficiente que genera señales de luz precisas usando solitones topológicos, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para tecnologías ópticas avanzadas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an elegant, glowing nanophotonic circuit shaped like intertwined waves emitting vibrant, evenly spaced light pulses symbolizing a frequency comb, with smooth, flowing ribbons representing topological solitons bridging two contrasting phases, all rendered in soft natural hues of amber, deep teal, and gentle ivory on a simple, dark background.

articles.title: Scientists just discovered bees and hummingbirds are drinking alcohol
articles.summary: Flower nectar often contains small amounts of alcohol, meaning pollinators like hummingbirds are drinking it all day long. Despite consuming human-equivalent amounts, they show no signs of intoxication—suggesting a surprising evolutionary tolerance.
articles.content:
As bees and hummingbirds move from one flower to another, feeding on nectar while helping plants reproduce, they are also consuming something unexpected: small amounts of alcohol.

In the first large survey of alcohol in floral nectar, biologists at the University of California, Berkeley detected ethanol in at least one sample from 26 of the 29 plant species they examined. Most nectar samples contained only trace amounts, likely produced by yeast fermenting sugars. However, one sample reached 0.056% ethanol by weight, which is about 1/10 proof.

How Much Alcohol Do Pollinators Consume

Although these levels sound tiny, nectar is a primary energy source for many species. Hummingbirds, for example, drink between 50% and 150% of their body weight in nectar each day. Based on these feeding habits, the researchers estimate that an Anna's hummingbird (Calypte anna), commonly found along the Pacific coast, consumes roughly 0.2 grams of ethanol per kilogram of body weight daily. That is comparable to a human having about one alcoholic drink.

Despite this regular intake, bees and birds consume the alcohol gradually throughout the day and do not show clear signs of intoxication. Earlier work by the same team found that hummingbirds will drink sugar water containing up to 1% alcohol, but they begin to avoid it when concentrations rise above that level.

Possible Effects Beyond Intoxication

Even so, nectar contains other compounds, such as nicotine and caffeine, that are known to influence animal behavior. Ethanol could have similar subtle effects.

"Hummingbirds are like little furnaces. They burn through everything really quick, so you don't expect anything to accumulate in their bloodstream," said doctoral student Aleksey Maro, who worked on the nectar analysis with postdoctoral fellow Ammon Corl. "But we don't know what kind of signaling or appetitive properties the alcohol has. There are other things that the ethanol could be doing aside from creating a buzz, like with humans."

"There may be other kinds of effects specific to the foraging biology of the species in question that could be beneficial," added Robert Dudley, UC Berkeley professor of integrative biology. "They're burning it so fast, I'm guessing that they probably aren't suffering inebriating effects. But it may also have other consequences for their behavior."

Maro, Corl and Dudley reported their findings March 25 in Royal Society Open Science, along with Berkeley colleagues Rauri Bowie and Jimmy McGuire, both professors of integrative biology and curators at the campus's Museum of Vertebrate Zoology.

Experiments Reveal Alcohol Tolerance

Earlier experiments conducted at a feeder outside Dudley's office showed that Anna's hummingbirds are largely indifferent to low alcohol concentrations in sugar water (below 1% by volume). However, when the concentration reaches 2%, they visit the feeder about half as often.

"Somehow they are metering their intake, so maybe zero to 1% is a more likely concentration that they would find in the wild than anything higher," he said.

Another study led by former graduate student Cynthia Wang-Claypool found that feathers, including those of Anna's hummingbirds, contain ethyl glucuronide, a byproduct of ethanol metabolism. This indicates that these birds not only ingest alcohol but process it in a way similar to mammals. Together, these findings suggest that birds and other animals, including human ancestors, may have evolved a tolerance for and sometimes even a preference for alcohol.

"The laboratory experiment was showing that yes, they will drink ethanol in their nectar, though they have some aversion to it if it gets too high," Corl said. "The feathers are saying that, yes, they will metabolize it. And then this study is saying that ethanol is actually pretty widespread in the nectar they consume."

Comparing Alcohol Intake Across Species

After measuring ethanol levels using an enzymatic assay, the team estimated daily alcohol intake for several nectar-feeding species based on their caloric needs. Because detailed feeding data are limited, they focused on two hummingbird species, including the Anna's hummingbird, and three species of sunbirds, which in South Africa feed on plants such as honeybush (Melianthus major). Sunbirds fill a similar ecological role in Africa as hummingbirds do in the Americas.

The researchers also compared these values with other animals, including the European honeybee, the pen-tailed tree shrew, fruit-eating chimpanzees, and humans consuming one standard drink per day (0.14 grams/kg/day). The tree shrew had the highest intake at 1.4 g/kg/day, while the honeybee had the lowest at 0.05 g/kg/day. Nectar-feeding birds fell in a similar range, consuming about 0.19 to 0.27 g/kg/day when feeding on native flowers.

Interestingly, the feeder experiments suggest that Anna's hummingbirds may ingest even more alcohol from fermented sugar water in feeders (0.30 g/kg/day) than from natural nectar.

Evolutionary Adaptations to Dietary Alcohol

This research is part of a broader five-year National Science Foundation project aimed at collecting genetic data from hummingbirds and sunbirds to understand how they adapt to different environments and food sources, including high altitudes, sugar-rich diets and frequently fermented nectar.

"These studies suggest that there may be a broad range of physiological adaptations across the animal kingdom to the ubiquity of dietary ethanol, and that the responses we see in humans may not be representative of all primates or of all animals generally," Dudley said. "Maybe there are other physiological detoxification pathways or other kinds of nutritional effects of ethanol for animals that are consuming it every day of their lives. That's the interesting thing -- this is chronic through the course of the day, but that's a lifetime exposure post-weaning. It just means that the comparative biology of ethanol ingestion deserves further study."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about evolutionary adaptations in pollinators consuming alcohol in nectar, revealing new insights into animal biology and metabolism with broad ecological and evolutionary implications. The research is detailed, focused, and offers meaningful advancement in understanding animal behavior and physiology.
articles.personality_title: Bees and hummingbirds regularly drink small amounts of alcohol from nectar without getting drunk
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many flowers produce nectar, a sugary liquid that animals like bees and hummingbirds drink to get energy. Scientists suspected nectar might contain tiny amounts of alcohol, but no big study had measured it across many plants.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California, Berkeley tested nectar from 29 plant species and found alcohol, called ethanol, in most of them. Although the amounts were small, some hummingbirds drink as much nectar daily as half to one and a half times their body weight. This means these birds consume alcohol amounts similar to one human drink every day. Experiments showed hummingbirds don’t get drunk from this and avoid nectar with higher alcohol levels.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that bees and hummingbirds have evolved to tolerate alcohol in their diet, unlike humans who get intoxicated. It also suggests that alcohol in nectar might affect animal behavior in ways we don’t fully understand yet. The finding challenges previous ideas about alcohol’s role in nature and opens new questions about how animals process it.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study the genetic and biological ways hummingbirds and similar birds handle alcohol. They want to learn if alcohol helps or changes how these animals find food and survive. This research may also reveal how other animals, including our ancestors, adapted to eating foods with natural alcohol.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Bees and hummingbirds regularly consume small amounts of alcohol in nectar without intoxication, showing a surprising natural tolerance shaped by evolution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les abeilles et les colibris boivent régulièrement de petites quantités d’alcool dans le nectar sans s’enivrer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses fleurs produisent du nectar, un liquide sucré que les animaux comme les abeilles et les colibris boivent pour obtenir de l’énergie. Les scientifiques pensaient que le nectar pouvait contenir de petites quantités d’alcool, mais aucune grande étude ne l’avait mesuré sur plusieurs plantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à Berkeley ont testé le nectar de 29 espèces de plantes et ont trouvé de l’alcool, appelé éthanol, dans la plupart d’entre elles. Bien que les quantités soient faibles, certains colibris boivent chaque jour autant de nectar que la moitié à une fois et demie leur poids corporel. Cela signifie que ces oiseaux consomment des quantités d’alcool similaires à un verre humain chaque jour. Des expériences ont montré que les colibris ne s’enivrent pas avec cela et évitent le nectar avec des concentrations plus élevées.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les abeilles et les colibris ont évolué pour tolérer l’alcool dans leur alimentation, contrairement aux humains qui s’enivrent. Elle suggère aussi que l’alcool dans le nectar pourrait influencer le comportement animal d’une manière encore mal comprise. Ce résultat remet en question les idées précédentes sur le rôle de l’alcool dans la nature et ouvre de nouvelles questions sur la façon dont les animaux le métabolisent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’étudier les mécanismes génétiques et biologiques que les colibris et oiseaux similaires utilisent pour gérer l’alcool. Ils veulent savoir si l’alcool aide ou change la façon dont ces animaux trouvent de la nourriture et survivent. Cette recherche pourrait aussi révéler comment d’autres animaux, y compris nos ancêtres, se sont adaptés à consommer des aliments contenant de l’alcool naturel.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les abeilles et les colibris consomment régulièrement de petites quantités d’alcool dans le nectar sans s’enivrer, révélant une tolérance naturelle étonnante façonnée par l’évolution.
articles.personality_title_es: Abejas y colibríes beben pequeñas cantidades de alcohol del néctar sin emborracharse
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas flores producen néctar, un líquido dulce que animales como las abejas y colibríes beben para obtener energía. Los científicos sospechaban que el néctar podría contener pequeñas cantidades de alcohol, pero no se había hecho un estudio grande que lo midiera en muchas plantas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en Berkeley probaron el néctar de 29 especies de plantas y encontraron alcohol, llamado etanol, en la mayoría. Aunque las cantidades eran pequeñas, algunos colibríes beben cada día tanto néctar como la mitad a una vez y media su peso corporal. Esto significa que estos pájaros consumen cantidades de alcohol similares a una bebida humana diaria. Experimentos mostraron que los colibríes no se emborrachan con esto y evitan el néctar con niveles más altos de alcohol.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que las abejas y colibríes han evolucionado para tolerar el alcohol en su dieta, a diferencia de los humanos que se intoxican. También sugiere que el alcohol en el néctar podría afectar el comportamiento animal de formas que aún no entendemos bien. El hallazgo desafía ideas anteriores sobre el papel del alcohol en la naturaleza y abre nuevas preguntas sobre cómo los animales lo procesan.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar las formas genéticas y biológicas en que los colibríes y aves similares manejan el alcohol. Quieren saber si el alcohol ayuda o cambia cómo estos animales encuentran comida y sobreviven. Esta investigación también podría revelar cómo otros animales, incluidos nuestros antepasados, se adaptaron a comer alimentos con alcohol natural.

**Resumen en una frase** – Abejas y colibríes consumen pequeñas cantidades de alcohol en el néctar sin emborracharse, mostrando una sorprendente tolerancia natural desarrollada por la evolución.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an Anna's hummingbird and a honeybee gently sipping nectar from vividly colored flowers with subtle, translucent bubbles of golden ethanol swirling around the nectar droplets, symbolizing their natural consumption of alcohol without intoxication.
